tickets.title: Fix cross-phase budget under-enforcement (spentBase stale after drainSpend)
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-reliability-budget-crossphase] createLLM() captures spentBase ONCE from
state.budget.spentUSD (src/llm.js:284) and checkBudget computes spent = spentBase + accumulated.costUSD
(llm.js:309). bin/cli.js creates ONE llm instance for the whole `do-better run` loop; every phase ends
with addSpend(state, PHASE_ID, llm.drainSpend()) and drainSpend() (llm.js:421-428) zeroes `accumulated`
while spentBase never advances. So after scan drains, comprehend's budget checks see none of scan's spend;
under --budget 10 with phases each spending $8 every phase passes checkBudget and the process spends $40+.
This silently breaks the D10 hard-ceiling / stop-the-world BudgetError doctrine the resume machinery depends on.

Design (decided): change spentBase from const to let; in drainSpend(), before zeroing, do
`spentBase += accumulated.costUSD`. checkBudget's running total (spentBase + accumulated.costUSD) is then
preserved across drains. No public-API change; sequential single-phase callers are unaffected (one drain
at phase end, spentBase already reflects it on the next phase's first check).

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: A single llm instance, two sequential spend-then-drain cycles, then checkBudget reflects the SUM of
  both drained costs (not just the current accumulator) — verified by: a new unit test in test/llm.test.js
  driving call() via DOBETTER_FAKE_LLM with a low --budget, asserting BudgetError fires on the cycle whose
  cumulative-across-drains spend crosses the ceiling (fails on main, passes after fix).
- AC2: Within a single phase (no drain), budget behavior is byte-identical to today — verified by:
  existing test/llm.test.js budget tests stay green.
- AC3: Full suite + T0 rails green — verified by: node --test.

tickets.title: Reservation-based budget check so concurrent calls cannot overshoot the cap
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-accelerate-budget-reservation] checkBudget (src/llm.js:306-318) is check-then-record:
it reads spent before the network call, recordSpend (320-326) lands after. This is already live-exploitable —
src/roadmap.js:546 runs `Promise.all(tickets.map(... repairTicket(ctx,t) ...))` (repairTicket = a real LLM
call via withFallback) so N concurrently-gapped tickets each pass the same budget snapshot and collectively
overshoot by up to (N-1)x per-call cost. It becomes categorical the moment H8 (concurrent finder pools /
D1 readers) ships. Silent overshoot violates the D10 hard-ceiling doctrine.

Design (decided): inside createLLM's closure add a `reserved` accumulator. checkBudget computes estCost
(already at 310-311), checks `spentBase + accumulated.costUSD + reserved + estCost > limit`, then adds
estCost to `reserved`. The call path must RELEASE the reservation in try/finally around the provider/fake
call — recordSpend records actuals AND subtracts the reservation; the retry-exhaustion OpError path and the
fake non-string throw (llm.js:395) must also release (finally handles all). A provable no-op for sequential
callers: reserved is always 0 at check time when calls don't overlap.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: Two overlapping call() promises against --budget where each alone fits but both together exceed →
  the second's checkBudget throws BudgetError (reservation seen) — verified by: a test/llm.test.js test with
  a fake whose function blocks on a shared gate until both callers have passed checkBudget.
- AC2: reserved returns to 0 after each call settles (success, provider error, and fake-throw) — verified by:
  a test asserting a later sequential call sees no phantom reservation after an errored concurrent batch.
- AC3: Sequential behavior byte-identical to H1 output — verified by: existing budget tests green.
- AC4: Full suite green.

Dependencies: H1 — contract: src/llm.js checkBudget/drainSpend (H1 lands the spentBase-promotion this builds on).

tickets.title: Retry classification + record spend on billed-but-unparseable responses (+ injectable sleep)
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-reliability-retry-classify][F-reliability-spend-leak] callProvider's retry loop
(src/llm.js:340-372) retries ALL errors 3x with fixed 1s/2s backoff: a permanent 400/401/403/422 burns ~3s
of dead sleep + re-issues doomed requests, and 429/529 ignore Retry-After. Worse, when res.ok is true (the
provider BILLED tokens) but req.parse(data) throws (llm.js:357 — realistic for Gemini safety-blocked
candidates with usageMetadata but no text, or OpenAI refusal shapes), the catch retries with ZERO
recordSpend — billed tokens never reach `accumulated`, so checkBudget and the persisted spentUSD undercount,
compounding H1/H2's ceiling doctrine.

Design (decided): (1) attach res.status to the thrown non-ok Error (llm.js:354); add isRetryable(err) —
retry only network errors, 408, 429, and 5xx; fail fast (throw OpError immediately) on other 4xx and on
parse errors. (2) In the billed-but-unparseable path, recordSpend the estimated (or data.usage) tokens
before deciding to fail — spend must never escape accounting. (3) Add an injectable `sleepImpl` to createLLM's
deps (alongside fetchImpl) so retry/backoff is testable without real wall-clock (unblocks H4).

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: A 401/400/422 response fails after exactly ONE request, no retry sleeps — verified by: test/llm.test.js
  fetchImpl call-count + sleepImpl spy.
- AC2: A network error / 503 retries up to RETRY_ATTEMPTS then throws the labelled OpError — verified by:
  fetchImpl-driven exhaustion test with injected sleepImpl (no real delay).
- AC3: res.ok true + req.parse throws → recordSpend was called (spend recorded, not lost) then the call
  fails/retries per classification — verified by: assert accumulated/drainSpend reflects the billed tokens.
- AC4: Full suite green.

Dependencies: none functional; H4 depends on this (sleepImpl seam).

tickets.title: Test coverage for callProvider retry/backoff loop
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-tests-retry-loop] callProvider (src/llm.js:340-372) is the reliability layer for
every online call and has zero retry coverage — the only response-returning fetchImpl test
(test/llm.test.js:131) is a first-try local success. Retry-then-succeed, exhaustion→labelled OpError
(369-371), the non-ok HTTP branch (352-355), and backoff doubling are all uncovered; a regression ships green.

Design (decided): using H3's injectable sleepImpl, add ~4 fetchImpl-driven tests. One test must PIN H3's
spend-recording fix (billed-but-unparseable → spend recorded), so the test survives as a regression guard.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: Retry-then-succeed on attempt 2 returns the text and records spend once — verified by: new test.
- AC2: Exhaustion throws OpError naming provider/model — verified by: new test.
- AC3: Backoff delays double (1000→2000) — verified by: sleepImpl spy captures the delay sequence.
- AC4: Full suite green fast (< a few seconds; no real sleeps).

Dependencies: H3 — contract: src/llm.js createLLM deps (sleepImpl seam + status classification).

tickets.title: Test coverage for anthropic/gemini request builders
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-tests-request-builders] buildRequest (src/llm.js:205-252) for anthropic and gemini
is never executed by any test (confirmed via node --test --experimental-test-coverage: lines 205-253
uncovered). The one provider:"anthropic" reference (test/llm.test.js:241) sets DOBETTER_FAKE_LLM so config
becomes "fake" and buildRequest is bypassed. Anthropic is the FIRST autodetected provider — every real user
run goes through untested code; a typo in URL/headers/body ships green.

Design (decided): createLLM({ flags:{provider:"anthropic"}, env:{ANTHROPIC_API_KEY:"k"}, fetchImpl }) with a
request-capturing fetchImpl that returns a canned provider-shaped body; assert URL, headers
(x-api-key/anthropic-version), body (system as top-level field), the parse() output, and jsonMode handling.
Mirror for gemini (systemInstruction, responseMimeType at 237, x-goog-api-key). No src/ change.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: Anthropic request URL/headers/body-shape asserted against a captured request — verified by: new test.
- AC2: Anthropic parse() returns {text,tokensIn,tokensOut} from a canned response — verified by: new test.
- AC3: Gemini request URL/headers/body (systemInstruction + responseMimeType when jsonMode) asserted —
  verified by: new test.
- AC4: Coverage of src/llm.js buildRequest anthropic+gemini branches goes from 0 to executed — verified by:
  node --test --experimental-test-coverage no longer lists 205-252 fully uncovered.
- AC5: Full suite green.

tickets.title: Semantic-dedupe judge and finder prior-list carry file:line
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-pipeline-dedupe-noline] isSemanticDuplicate (src/identify.js:572) builds its prompt
from title+claim only (575-581), and the finder prior-conclusions list (687-690) is title+file only. Two
genuinely distinct defects of the same class in one file — e.g. two swallowed catch blocks at lines 120 and
480 — produce near-identical title/claim text, so the cheap judge (DEDUPE_SYSTEM) has NO signal to separate
"paraphrase of the same issue" from "same class at a different location". The code's own doctrine (566-570)
and SKILL.md declare a false duplicate permanently unrecoverable. priorFixed from readFindings (915-917)
already drops evidence[0].line even though it exists (491); every candidate carries a required validated
`line` (148-170). Pure prompt plumbing of data already in hand.

Design (decided): (1) priorByDim entries (915-917) include `line: prior.evidence?.[0]?.line ?? null`.
(2) isSemanticDuplicate's list (575-577) renders `${file}:${line}` per prior entry, and the new-finding
block includes the candidate's file:line. (3) finderCell priorList (687-690) renders `${title} (${file}:${line})`.
(4) Update SKILL.md D2 step 2 wording to note location is part of the duplicate judgment.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: Two candidates, same dimension+file+title/claim wording but different lines → the second is NOT
  suppressed (admitted as distinct) when the scripted dedupe fake keys off the line in the prompt — verified
  by: a test in test/identify-dedupe.test.js asserting the dedupe prompt CONTAINS both file:line pairs and
  the second candidate becomes a finding.
- AC2: A true paraphrase at the SAME file:line is still suppressed — verified by: existing dedupe paraphrase
  test stays green.
- AC3: T0 rails (test/identify-rails.test.js) + full suite green — verified by: node --test.

tickets.title: Declare deep-read truncation past SLICE_CHARS in the D2 coverage manifest
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-pipeline-silent-truncation] loadSlices (src/identify.js:258-267) hard-truncates each
file to SLICE_CHARS=24,000 at read time; both consumers (renderSlice/LLM packets and the offline regex
finders at 351) read the truncated s.raw, so content past 24k is never examined by ANY (dimension × packet)
cell and partitionSlices' "every slice in exactly one packet" guarantee covers only file heads. The
truncation is usually UNDECLARED: truncatedFiles (line 781) tests renderSlice(s).length > PACKET_BYTES
(30,000) against a raw already capped at 24,000, so it only fires when line-number overhead exceeds 6KB.
Empirically, identify.js itself (53,675 chars) truncates to lines 1-498, renders at 26,408 < 30,000, and
the manifest declares "Truncated slices: (none)" while >50% of the file was never audited — a direct breach
of the declared-never-silent doctrine (D10).

Design (decided): loadSlices records `truncated: raw.length > SLICE_CHARS` (and optionally fullChars) on
each slice before truncating. truncatedFiles (781) becomes
`slices.filter((s) => s.truncated || renderSlice(s).length > PACKET_BYTES)` so BOTH read-time and
packet-render truncation are declared. The "## D2 finder coverage" manifest section already lists truncated
slices; this ticket makes that list correct. (Splitting oversized files into multiple examined slices is a
follow-on, F-pipeline-truncation-split / medium — this ticket DECLARES honestly; it does not yet increase
coverage. Note that in verification.md.)

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: A deep-read file whose content exceeds SLICE_CHARS appears in truncatedFiles / the manifest's
  truncated list — verified by: a test in test/identify-coverage.test.js with a >24k fixture file asserting
  it is declared truncated (fails on main, passes after fix).
- AC2: A small file rendered under PACKET_BYTES is NOT declared truncated — verified by: same test, negative case.
- AC3: verification.md notes read-time truncation is declared but not yet split-covered — verified by:
  reviewer grep for "truncat" in verification.md.
- AC4: T0 rails + full suite green.

tickets.title: Concurrent pooled finder calls (D2) and independent reader/behavior calls (D1)
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-accelerate-finder-pool-seq][F-accelerate-d1-serial] Supersedes v0.2 ticket T6. In
finderCell (src/identify.js:674-745) the per-pass prompt/priorList are built ONCE before the pool loop
(687-696) and the poolWidth jsonCall calls (705) differ only by lens — fully independent, yet awaited one at
a time; per-response validation/dedupe does not feed back into the SAME pass. In comprehend.js the per-packet
behavior-inventory calls (396-413) and the five reader artifacts (427-443) are independent (rails-map's only
input, behaviors, is complete before any reader runs), yet serial. Parallelizing cuts D2 wall-clock ~poolWidth×
for high-weight dimensions and D1 ~3-5×, at ZERO cost and ZERO semantic change.

Design (decided): add zero-dep order-preserving `mapLimit(items, limit, fn)` to src/utils.js (first
rejection propagates after in-flight settle). Apply to (a) the poolWidth finder calls WITHIN a pass —
process responses in lens-index order so seen/pool/semantic-dedupe admission order is byte-identical to
sequential; (b) D1's per-packet behavior calls and the independent readers. Dimensions and packets stay
SEQUENTIAL (spend ordering, log readability, and the pass-level dry-loop doctrine — prior pool conclusions
feed the NEXT pass). DO NOT parallelize per-candidate verifyCandidate: ctx.exec is spawnSync (utils.js:292)
which blocks the event loop, so concurrent verify would serialize on git/repro subprocesses anyway and stall
sibling fetches — out of scope until the exec layer is async (separate ticket). Requires H2's reservation so
the budget ceiling holds under concurrency.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: mapLimit unit tests — order preserved, concurrency ≤ limit (counter probe), first rejection
  propagates — verified by: test/utils.test.js.
- AC2: Identify findings/state byte-identical between a forced limit=1 and the concurrent path on the same
  scripted fake — verified by: test/identify.test.js determinism assertion.
- AC3: D1 behaviors + reader artifacts byte-identical between serial and concurrent on the same fake —
  verified by: test/comprehend.test.js.
- AC4: Budget ceiling still holds under concurrent finder calls — verified by: a --budget test that would
  overshoot without H2's reservation.
- AC5: T0 rails + full suite green.

Dependencies: H2 — contract: src/llm.js reservation-based checkBudget.

tickets.title: Close the vacuous-repro verification hole (relatedness / blind-reread requirement)
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-pipeline-vacuous-repro] verifyCandidate (src/identify.js:486) treats ANY whitelisted
repro that exits 0 as proof: runRepro returns exitCode 0 → verified with method "command", skipping the F2
blind reread entirely. Nothing checks the proposed check relates to the claim. The mid-tier repro proposer
(REPRO_SYSTEM, line 30) can return `node -e "1"` (any non-empty snippet ≤500 chars passes sanitizeRepro
448-455 and exits 0) or `{"grep":{"pattern":".","file":<cited file>}}` (matches every non-empty file), and
the finding is verified without the frontier verifier ever running — the weakest model in the chain defeats
the F2 defense. verification.md:49-51 requires "the command demonstrating the claim succeeds"; the code
enforces only "a command succeeds".

Design (decided): coordinate with H-eval (T7 successor) — with eval-repro OFF by default a bare `node -e`
never runs. Beyond that: (1) reject catch-all grep patterns (`.`, `.*`, empty) in sanitizeRepro/staticCheckSpec
as non-demonstrative; (2) require that even a passing command repro is CONFIRMED by the blind frontier reread
(or that the repro output is asserted to reference the cited symbol/line), so a trivially-passing check cannot
alone flip a candidate to verified. Keep verifier blindness (proposer reasoning withheld).

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: A candidate whose repro is `node -e "1"` (eval off) or a catch-all grep does NOT verify on the repro
  alone — verified by: test/identify.test.js scripted fake asserting the candidate falls through to blind
  reread (or is killed), not method:"command" verified.
- AC2: A genuine demonstrative repro (grep for the actual cited pattern that matches) still verifies —
  verified by: existing repro-path test stays green.
- AC3: verification.md documents the relatedness requirement — verified by: reviewer grep.
- AC4: T0 rails + full suite green.

tickets.title: Detect a dirty working tree before minting @sha citations (D-1/D1/D2)
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-reliability-dirty-tree] Only rail.js checks git status --porcelain (rail.js:80-81),
and only as a warn at D4. scan, comprehend, and identify all read the WORKING TREE (loadSlices, enrichFacts,
verifyCitations use fs on checkout files) but stamp every citation/evidence record with @headSha from
git rev-parse HEAD (identify.js:761, writeFinding evidence pinned to head7). On a dirty tree every
file:line@sha claim is attributed to a commit that does not contain that content, refresh's changedFilesSince
can't see those edits once committed under the same lines, and reproduction replays against content the sha
never had. Violates SPEC.md:82/91 "all claims pinned to commit SHA" and the declared-never-silent doctrine.
The project already logged this as a round-4 finding (identify.js:269-283) but only defends its resume cache.

Design (decided): add a shared `workingTreeStatus(root, exec)` helper to src/utils.js; at the START of
scan/comprehend/identify emit a DECLARED warning (and record it in the coverage manifest / gate record) when
the tree is dirty — "citations pinned to <sha> but the working tree has uncommitted changes; claims may not
match the committed blob." Warn-level (not a hard gate) to preserve the personal-use degrade path, but never
silent. Optionally add a strict `--require-clean` flag that promotes it to exit-2.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: On a dirty tree, scan emits the declared dirty-tree warning and records it — verified by:
  test/scan.test.js with an uncommitted file asserting the warning + manifest note.
- AC2: On a clean tree, no warning — verified by: same test, clean case.
- AC3: Behavior otherwise unchanged (citations still minted) — verified by: existing scan tests green.
- AC4: Full suite green.

tickets.title: scan.js must not lose paid LLM spend when writeArtifact/recordPhase throws
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-reliability-scan-spend-loss] scan's run() wraps only the LLM call in try/catch
(src/scan.js:278-293); the drain into state happens later at line 300. If writeArtifact (295) or recordPhase
throws AFTER a successful PAID codemap call (disk full, permissions, bad LAYOUT path), the error propagates
with no err.state attached, cli.js's catch saves the pre-call state, and the codemap call's cost vanishes
from budget.spentUSD forever — compounding H1's undercount. Every other phase already uses a whole-body catch
that attaches err.state = addSpend(state, PHASE_ID, llm.drainSpend()) (charter.js:653-655, identify.js:1046-1050,
comprehend.js:527-531).

Design (decided): extend scan's try to cover writeArtifact + addSpend + recordPhase + pinSha (the whole body
after collecting facts), and in the catch attach err.state = addSpend(state, PHASE_ID, llm.drainSpend()) if
not already set — mirroring the other phases. Keep the fail-closed semantics (still throws).

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: writeArtifact throwing after a paid codemap call → the thrown error carries err.state whose
  budget.spentUSD includes the codemap spend — verified by: test/scan.test.js injecting a write failure
  (e.g. read-only dotdir / a stubbed writer) and asserting err.state spend (fails on main, passes after fix).
- AC2: Happy path unchanged — verified by: existing scan tests green.
- AC3: Full suite green.

tickets.title: refresh: correct asOf sha for stale markers + unify citation regex with artifacts.js
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-reliability-refresh-asof] Two silent-degradation bugs. (Bug A, primary)
reverifyFindings (src/refresh.js:129) stamps stale-finding markers with asOfSha = pins.refresh ?? headSha,
but the changed-file diff base is pins.comprehend ?? pins.scan (refresh.js:190), and the correct sibling
flagStaleArtifacts already uses `pinned` (refresh.js:70). On first refresh the marker claims "changed since
<current HEAD>" (vacuously false); on later refreshes it cites the last refresh sha, also wrong since the
diff base never advances. (Bug B, secondary) artifacts.js CITATION_RE accepts uppercase hex
[0-9a-fA-F]{7,40} (artifacts.js:242) but repinText (refresh.js:59), reverifyInventory (107-108) and the
evidence re-pin (157) use lowercase-only [0-9a-f]{7,40}; an uppercase-SHA citation is silently skipped by
re-pin, staying pinned to a stale sha with no warning.

Design (decided): (A) thread `pinned` (the diff base already computed in run(), refresh.js:190) into
reverifyFindings and use it for asOfSha instead of pins.refresh. (B) export a single CITATION_RE (and a
small parse helper) from src/artifacts.js and use it in all four refresh.js sites so SHA-case handling is
uniform.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: On first refresh, a stale finding's ">" marker cites the diff-base sha (pins.comprehend/scan), not
  current HEAD — verified by: test/refresh.test.js asserting the annotated body's asOf sha equals the diff
  base (fails on main for Bug A, passes after fix).
- AC2: An uppercase-SHA citation is re-pinned, not skipped — verified by: a refresh test with an uppercase
  @SHA citation asserting it advances.
- AC3: Full suite green.

tickets.title: Test coverage for refresh.js reread re-verdict path (RESOLVED stamping)
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-tests-refresh-reread] reverifyFindings' method==="reread" branch (src/refresh.js:131-145)
asks the frontier LLM for CONFIRM/KILL/UNCERTAIN and on KILL permanently stamps the finding "> RESOLVED"
(151-154) — nothing downstream resurrects a resolved finding, so a regression here silently drops live
findings from the roadmap. Confirmed via coverage: refresh.js lines 48-53 (coerceJson) and 132-145 (the
reread branch) are entirely uncovered. reread findings are produced in production by identify.js:541 (blind
frontier reread), so this is a live path, not dead code.

Design (decided): add scripted-"verdict" fake tests: (1) CONFIRM re-pins evidence to the new sha via
repinText and clears stale; (2) KILL writes the "> RESOLVED @ <sha>" stamp + stale:true and adds the id to
resolvedIds; (3) UNCERTAIN leaves the finding stale with no resolve; (4) a verdict arriving fenced in
```json exercises coerceJson (48-53). No src/ change.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: KILL → finding body contains "> RESOLVED", meta.stale true, id in resolvedIds — verified by: new test.
- AC2: CONFIRM → evidence re-pinned to the new sha, stale false — verified by: new test.
- AC3: UNCERTAIN → stays stale, not resolved — verified by: new test.
- AC4: Fenced JSON verdict is parsed (coerceJson covered) — verified by: new test.
- AC5: Full suite green.

tickets.title: Test coverage for coldstart gate degradations and ticket-demotion fail-closed paths
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-tests-coldstart-gaps] Three coldstart-adjacent gate branches in src/roadmap.js are
uncovered: (1) the offline static-lint degradation (458-464) — coldstart absent AND llm.offline routes gaps
through validateTicket with degraded:"static-lint"; no run() test sets llm.offline=true. (2) a FAILING
native probe — no test scripts coldstart-probe returning pass:false, so gaps → repair loop → GateError is
unexercised. (3) the invalid-after-repair demotion (526-533) — no test drives validateTicket to fail twice,
so the demotedIds "move to Later" path (a finding losing its ticket) never runs. All three are fail-closed
gate behaviors whose regression would silently pass bad tickets to execution.

Design (decided): add three roadmap.test.js integration tests via the fake seam — offline degradation,
failing-probe→GateError exit 2, and double-invalid→demotion. No src/ change.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: Offline + coldstart-absent → gaps validated via static-lint, degradation declared — verified by: new test.
- AC2: Native probe pass:false → repair loop runs, then GateError with exit-2 semantics — verified by: new test.
- AC3: A ticket invalid after one repair round → demoted to Later, id in demotedIds — verified by: new test.
- AC4: Full suite green.

tickets.title: Extract a shared phase-runtime module; unify the three divergent JSON-salvage impls
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-refactoring-phase-runtime] Five phase modules re-implement the same runtime helpers:
patchPhase ×4 (comprehend:113, identify:131, rail:126, refresh:28), gateFail/makeGateError ×5 (comprehend:117,
identify:135, rail:118, roadmap:193, refresh:40), byte-identical jsonCall ×2 (comprehend:179, identify:221),
loadRef/refText ×3 (identify:203, roadmap:176, rail:107), and the drainSpend-attach-to-err.state catch
pattern ×7. Drift is ACTIVE not hypothetical: coerceJson has already forked into two divergent copies
(roadmap:180 throws OpError, refresh:48 returns null), and every gateFail copy misuses the
GateError(gate, detail) constructor (utils.js:69) by passing a pre-formatted message as the gate arg,
producing garbled "Gate failed: X — X" messages — a live bug class the extraction eliminates.

Design (decided): extract patchPhase, makeGateError (using the constructor correctly), jsonCall, loadRef,
and ONE canonical coerceJson (decide throw-vs-null; document) into src/utils.js (or a new src/phase-runtime.js);
replace the duplicated call sites. Zero behavior change EXCEPT the GateError message fix (call it out).
Scope is broad but mechanical; land after the other identify/refresh/roadmap tickets to avoid churn conflicts.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: One exported coerceJson/jsonCall/patchPhase/makeGateError; no duplicate local copies remain —
  verified by: grep shows single definitions.
- AC2: GateError messages are well-formed ("Gate failed: <gate> — <detail>", no doubled text) — verified by:
  a test/utils.test.js assertion on a constructed gate failure message.
- AC3: T0 rails + full suite byte-behavior green (except the corrected message) — verified by: node --test.

Dependencies: sequence after H6/H7/H9/H12/H14 (shared-file churn).

tickets.title: Per-cell progress + running spend for the D1 comprehend and D2 identify phases
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-dx-silent-phases] The two longest phases print nothing while working: identify.js
run() (750-1052) and finderCell (674-745) loop over dims×packets×passes×poolWidth LLM calls plus
per-candidate verify with no log.phase/log.step (only warn/substep on suppress/kill), and comprehend.js
run() (347-533) is equally silent. Only charter.js (D0) and scan.js (D-1) — the two CHEAPEST phases — use
log.phase/log.step. On a real repo this is tens of minutes of dead console.

Design (decided): (a) log.phase("D2","Identify") / log.phase("D1","Comprehend") at run() entry;
(b) a per-cell log.step in the D2 packet loop (identify.js:976-996), e.g.
"[security] packet 2/5 · pass N · 4 verified/7 killed · $1.83 spent" — dims.length, packets.length, pi,
counts, and a running spend accessor are all in scope (add a lightweight llm.spentSoFar() or read the
accumulator); (c) a per-packet / per-reader log.step in comprehend. Output only (no logic change); must be
gated so --json does not pollute stdout (coordinate with H17 if landed).

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: A D2 run emits at least one progress line per (dimension × packet) cell with dimension, packet index,
  and running verified/killed counts — verified by: test/identify.test.js capturing a log spy.
- AC2: D1 emits a progress line per reader/packet — verified by: comprehend log spy (or manual if no spy seam).
- AC3: No change to findings/state/gates — verified by: existing identify/comprehend tests green.
- AC4: Full suite green.

tickets.title: Machine-consumable --json: error envelopes, richer success payload, stdout hygiene
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-dx-json-incomplete] Three CI gaps: (1) the JSON envelope is emitted only on success
(bin/cli.js:198-200); the outer catch (224-233) prints human text to stderr — a CI wrapper gets no
{ok:false, gate, detail} for GateError/OpError/BudgetError, forcing exit-code-only integration. (2) the
success envelope is just {command, ok, paused, summary} — no spend (state.budget.spentUSD), verified/killed
counts, gate name, or artifact paths, all sitting in `state`. (3) phase modules write decorative output to
stdout unconditionally (log.phase/step/success/substep → console.log), so with --json, stdout mixes
human lines with the JSON object and is not parseable.

Design (decided): (1) in the outer catch, when flags.json, print a JSON error envelope
{ok:false, command, error:{kind, gate?, detail}} to stdout and exit with the right code. (2) enrich the
success envelope with spend + counts + gate + artifact paths from state. (3) when flags.json, route
log.phase/step/substep/success to stderr (or suppress) so stdout carries ONLY the final JSON.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: A GateError under --json prints a parseable {ok:false,...} on stdout with exit 2 — verified by:
  test/cli.test.js spawn asserting JSON.parse(stdout).ok === false and exit code.
- AC2: A successful --json run's envelope includes spend and verified/killed counts — verified by: spawn test.
- AC3: Under --json, stdout is a single JSON object (no interleaved human lines) — verified by: spawn test
  asserting JSON.parse(stdout) succeeds on the whole stream.
- AC4: Non-json output unchanged — verified by: existing cli tests green.
- AC5: Full suite green.

Dependencies: coordinate with H16 (both touch phase logging / stdout routing).

tickets.title: Fix docs drift: README/SPEC/SKILL vs shipped behavior
tickets.body:
Motivation: [F-dx-docs-drift] Confirmed drift: (1) README's env-var table (159-167) omits
DOBETTER_LOCAL_BASE_URL / DOBETTER_LOCAL_MODEL / DOBETTER_LOCAL_API_KEY (and DOBETTER_FAKE_LLM), all in
HELP_TEXT (utils.js:236-245) and required for the --provider local path the README's own install section
advertises. (2) README's D2 sizing table row for N (line 151) still says "a variable here; charter-weighted
pooling lands in T2" — T2 shipped (charterPoolWidth, identify.js:121) and is described 40 lines earlier in
the same README, so the doc contradicts itself. (3) SPEC.md §4 (line 109) lists
--provider anthropic|gemini|openai and omits `local`, which is implemented and documented elsewhere.

Design (decided): (1) add the DOBETTER_LOCAL_* rows to the README env-var table (DOBETTER_FAKE_LLM optional,
mark as test seam). (2) update README line 151 to state pooling is shipped (present tense), consistent with
lines 105-117. (3) add `local` to SPEC.md §4's provider list. Docs-only.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: README env-var table lists DOBETTER_LOCAL_BASE_URL/MODEL/API_KEY — verified by: grep in README.md.
- AC2: README no longer says pooling "lands in T2" in future tense — verified by: grep returns no
  "lands in T2".
- AC3: SPEC.md §4 provider list includes local — verified by: grep in SPEC.md.
- AC4: No code/test change; full suite green.
